questions.question: Explain the role of the Bootstrap Program in the operating system boot process.
keyPoints: Stored in ROM/EEPROM
First program to execute at power-on
Performs POST to check hardware
Locates and loads OS kernel into memory
Transfers control to kernel after loading

questions.question: What are system calls? Explain why they are necessary in operating systems.
keyPoints: Interface between user processes and kernel
Provide controlled access to privileged operations
Ensure system security and stability
Examples: fork, exec, read, write
Involves mode switch from user to kernel mode

questions.question: Describe the concept of Dual-Mode Operation in operating systems.
keyPoints: User Mode and Kernel Mode
Protects critical system resources
Privileged instructions only in kernel mode
Prevents unauthorized hardware access
Mode switch via system calls or interrupts

questions.question: Compare Monolithic Kernel and Microkernel architectures.
keyPoints: Monolithic: all services in kernel space (Linux, Unix)
Microkernel: minimal services in kernel (Minix, QNX)
Monolithic: faster but less reliable
Microkernel: more secure and reliable but slower
Hybrid approaches combine both (Windows NT)

questions.question: List and explain five major services provided by an operating system.
keyPoints: Process Management: creating, scheduling, terminating processes
Memory Management: allocating/deallocating memory
File Management: creating, deleting, organizing files
Device Management: controlling input/output devices
Security and Protection: user authentication, access control

questions.question: What is an interrupt? Explain its role in operating system operation.
keyPoints: Signal to CPU requiring immediate attention
Hardware interrupts from devices
Software interrupts from system calls
CPU saves state and transfers to handler
Enables efficient handling of asynchronous events

questions.question: Explain the difference between a system call and a function call.
keyPoints: System call involves mode switch to kernel
Function call stays within user space
System calls provide OS services
Function calls are part of program/library code
System calls have higher overhead due to context switch

questions.question: Explain the operating system boot process from power-on to user login. Include all major steps.
keyPoints: Power Supply: Voltage stabilization
BIOS/UEFI: Power-On Self Test (POST)
BIOS: Search and execute boot loader from MBR/UEFI
Boot Loader: Loads kernel from storage to memory
Kernel: Initializes hardware subsystems
Kernel: Starts init process (systemd/init)
Runlevel/Target: Starts system services
Display Manager: Shows login screen
User authentication grants access

questions.question: Describe the layered architecture of an operating system. Explain the functions of each layer and discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Hardware Layer: Physical devices, CPU, memory, I/O
Device Driver Layer: Interface to hardware devices
Kernel Layer: Process management, memory management, I/O
System Call Interface: API for user programs
Application Layer: User programs and utilities
Advantages: Simplicity, maintainability, abstraction
Disadvantages: Performance overhead, complex layering can be inefficient
Examples: Traditional UNIX, early OS/2

questions.question: What is a device driver? Explain how device drivers work with the operating system and hardware.
keyPoints: Device driver is kernel software component
Translates OS requests to device commands
Handles device initialization and configuration
Manages interrupts from devices
Provides uniform interface to applications
Examples: Network card drivers, storage drivers
Can be loaded at boot or dynamically (hot-plugging)
Runs in kernel mode with full privileges

questions.question: Explain the concept of Inter-Process Communication (IPC). Discuss at least four different IPC mechanisms.
keyPoints: IPC allows processes to exchange data and synchronize
Pipes: Unidirectional byte stream between related processes
Message Queues: Structured message passing with priority
Shared Memory: Multiple processes access common memory region
Sockets: Network-based or local communication
Signals: Asynchronous notifications between processes
Semaphores: Synchronization primitive for resource access
RPC: Remote Procedure Call for distributed systems
